title: Sentence & Paragraph-Aware Chunking
concept: Splitting on natural language boundaries so chunks never cut a sentence in half.

exercises.prompt: In the last lesson, we learned about fixed-size chunking. What is its main limitation?

options.text: It analyzes the meaning of sentences to group similar ideas together.
It splits text at a strict character or token limit, often cutting words in half.
It automatically translates text into multiple languages to expand the dataset.

exercises.explain: Fixed-size chunking splits text at a set number of characters or tokens, which regularly results in words and sentences being cut in half.

cards.title: Don't Cut Me Off!
cards.body: Have you ever read a book where pages were torn randomly in the middle of a sentence? It's confusing for you, and it's just as confusing for an LLM trying to find answers in your database.

cards.title: What is Boundary-Aware Chunking?
cards.body: Sentence and paragraph-aware chunking uses natural punctuation like periods, question marks, and newlines to split documents. Instead of cutting mid-word, it ensures each chunk contains complete, self-contained thoughts.

cards.title: The Book-Slicer Analogy
cards.body: Imagine a book printed with a paper cutter slicing exactly every three inches. Some words and sentences will be cut right in half, leaving nonsense. Paragraph-aware chunking is like a clean book editor who only cuts at the ends of chapters, paragraphs, or completed sentences.

cards.title: Sentences Kept Whole
cards.body: If your text is "Cats are nice. They like to nap.", a fixed-size splitter might cut at "Cats are ni" and "ce. They like to nap." A sentence-aware splitter keeps "Cats are nice." as one complete chunk.

cards.title: Myth: LLMs Don't Mind Broken Sentences
cards.body: Many believe that LLMs can easily piece together split sentences using surrounding context. In reality, chopped sentences degrade embedding quality and make retrieved chunks harder for the LLM to understand.

cards.title: Fixed vs. Sentence-Aware
cards.body: See how splitting on punctuation keeps thoughts clean, while splitting on character counts cuts words in half.

left.text: "AI is chang" | "ing the world." (Cuts the word 'changing' in half)

right.text: "AI is changing the world." | "It helps us learn." (Keeps sentences complete)

exercises.prompt: What is the primary advantage of sentence-aware chunking over simple fixed-size chunking?

options.text: It is much faster to calculate because it ignores all grammar rules.
It preserves complete thoughts, making the retrieved context clean and easy for the LLM to read.
It guarantees that every single chunk has the exact same character count.
It automatically translates foreign languages before storing them in the vector database.

exercises.explain: Fixed-size chunking splits text at strict character or token counts, often cutting sentences or words in half. Sentence-aware chunking respects grammar boundaries.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Sentence-aware chunkers use punctuation marks as guides to split text instead of relying solely on character counts.
exercises.explain: Sentence-aware chunkers look for punctuation marks (like '.', '!', '?') and newlines to find natural breaking points instead of counting character lengths blindly.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing word to complete the description of boundary-aware chunking:
exercises.sentence: Sentence-aware chunking relies on natural punctuation to decide where one chunk ends and the next begins.
exercises.explain: Punctuation marks like periods, question marks, and newlines act as natural boundaries to prevent splitting sentences in half.

exercises.prompt: Which of these represents an implementation of paragraph-aware chunking?

options.text: A database query that pulls the first 500 characters of a document using a SQL SUBSTRING command.
A Python function: `chunks = text.split('\n\n')` to split a document into separate paragraphs.
A script that removes all spaces from a text document and saves it as a single long line.

exercises.explain: Sentence-aware chunking uses natural boundaries like periods or double newlines to keep sentences and paragraphs intact, which is exactly what the Python code does by splitting on double newlines.

exercises.prompt: You are designing a chunking pipeline for a legal contract search engine. To keep clauses and terms from losing their meaning, you choose a paragraph-aware splitter. How does this algorithm prioritize its cut points?

options.text: It splits at double newlines (\n\n) first, keeping paragraphs intact if they fit under the character limit.
It counts exactly 100 characters and inserts a hard split, ignoring formatting.
It translates all text into tokens and splits only when a mathematical formula is completed.
It randomly shuffles sentences to balance the token weight across all chunks.
